name: Córdoba - Villaviciosa de Córdoba Loop
name_original: Villaviciosa de Córdoba — circular desde Córdoba
description: This difficult 58.2-mile (93.6 km) road cycling route from Córdoba offers a challenging ride through the Sierra Morena with significant elev
region_intro: No traffic road cycling routes around the Guadalquivir traverse a diverse landscape, from flat riverbanks to rolling hills and natural parks. The region is characterized by extensive olive groves, Mediterranean forests, and fertile farmland, offering varied scenery for cyclists. Routes often follow the Guadalquivir River, providing views of historical cities and natural features. This blend of terrain caters to a range of cycling abilities, from leisurely rides to more challenging ascents. Best no traffic road cycling routes around the Guadalquivir The most popular no traffic road cycling route is Córdoba - Villaviciosa de Córdoba Loop, a 58.2 miles (93.6 km) trail that takes 5 hours 0 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through varied landscapes north of Córdoba. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ascent to the Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza — circular route through the Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar, a difficult 46.7 miles (75.2 km) path. This route offers challenging climbs and scenic views within the natural park. Local road cyclists also love the Route of the Swamps — Sierra Morena — circular from Alcolea de Córdoba, a 35.4 miles (57.0 km) trail leading through the Sierra Morena, often completed in about 2 hours 59 minutes. Road cycling around the Guadalquivir is defined by extensive olive groves, Mediterranean forests, and riverside paths. The network offers options for different ability levels, from flat valley rides to routes with significant elevation changes. The routes in Guadalquivir are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 150 reviews. More than 14,500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadalquivir's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no-traffic road cycling routes are available in the Guadalquivir region?
A: There are nearly 90 dedicated no-traffic road cycling routes around the Guadalquivir region listed on komoot. These routes offer a variety of experiences, from easy rides to more challenging ascents, ensuring options for all skill levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on no-traffic road cycling routes in Guadalquivir?
A: The Guadalquivir region offers a diverse range of landscapes for no-traffic road cycling. You can expect to ride through extensive olive groves, Mediterranean forests, and fertile farmlands. Many routes follow the picturesque Guadalquivir River, especially around cities like Seville and Córdoba, providing scenic views and a tranquil experience.

Q: Are there any challenging no-traffic road cycling routes with significant elevation?
A: Yes, the Guadalquivir region offers several challenging no-traffic road cycling routes with significant elevation. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005725803/cordoba-villaviciosa-de-cordoba-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Córdoba - Villaviciosa de Córdoba Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route covering over 93 km with more than 1800 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1273132029/ascent-to-the-santuario-virgen-de-la-cabeza-circular-route-through-the-parque-natural-sierra-de-andujar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza&lt;/a&gt;, a circular route through the Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar, which features over 1150 meters of climbing.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see along no-traffic road cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region?
A: Many no-traffic road cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region pass by significant historical landmarks. In Seville, you might cycle past the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5343886" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tower of Gold&lt;/a&gt; or the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/215404" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seville Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;. Near Córdoba, routes can lead you close to the magnificent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4041503" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mosque–Cathedral of Córdoba&lt;/a&gt; or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3176628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcázar of the Christian Monarchs&lt;/a&gt;. The region is rich in heritage, with routes often integrating these cultural highlights.

Q: Are there any circular no-traffic road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, there are several circular no-traffic road cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005731784/route-of-the-swamps-sierra-morena-circular-from-alcolea-de-cordoba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Swamps — Sierra Morena&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate circular ride starting from Alcolea de Córdoba. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995742932/sierra-de-hornachuelos-loop-from-penaflor" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Hornachuelos Loop from Peñaflor&lt;/a&gt;, providing a scenic moderate circular tour.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about no-traffic cycling in Guadalquivir?
A: The no-traffic road cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 out of 5 stars from over 160 ratings. Reviewers often praise the region's diverse terrain, the tranquility of cycling through olive groves and along riverbanks, and the opportunity to combine natural beauty with historical exploration.

Q: What is the best time of year to go no-traffic road cycling in Guadalquivir?
A: The best time for no-traffic road cycling in the Guadalquivir region is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is generally mild and pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, sometimes wetter, conditions of winter. This allows for comfortable rides through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any no-traffic road cycling routes that pass through natural parks?
A: Yes, some no-traffic road cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region traverse or border natural parks, offering beautiful natural scenery. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996078062/andujar-tour-around-puerto-de-la-cadena-parque-natural-sierra-de-andujar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Andujar - tour around Puerto de La Cadena&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through the Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar, known for its rugged and wild landscapes.

Q: Can I find easy no-traffic road cycling routes suitable for beginners?
A: Yes, the Guadalquivir region offers easy no-traffic road cycling routes suitable for beginners. These routes often follow flatter sections along the riverbanks or through less hilly agricultural areas. While specific easy routes are not listed here, komoot features 5 easy routes in the region, perfect for those new to road cycling or looking for a relaxed ride.

Q: Are there any specific routes that offer views of reservoirs or significant water bodies?
A: While not explicitly detailed for every no-traffic route, the broader Andalusia region, which encompasses the Guadalquivir, includes routes that offer scenic reservoir views. The 'Route of the Swamps' mentioned earlier, for instance, suggests proximity to water bodies, providing picturesque backdrops for your ride.

Q: What are some notable towns or villages that no-traffic road cycling routes might pass through?
A: No-traffic road cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region often connect or pass near charming towns and villages. Beyond the major cities of Seville and Córdoba, you might encounter places like Carmona, known for its baroque palaces and hilltop views, or Palma del Río, with its historical significance. These towns offer opportunities to experience local culture and history during your ride.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Villaviciosa de Córdoba — circular from Córdoba route?
faqs.answer: This is a difficult road cycling route, classified as a 'Hard road ride'. It demands very good fitness due to its substantial elevation gain of 1,830 meters over 93.6 kilometers. Cyclists should be prepared for challenging terrain, including potential unpaved segments where pushing your bike might be necessary.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting from Córdoba. While specific parking details for the exact trailhead are not provided, you would typically look for suitable parking within Córdoba city that allows for easy access to the start of the route. Consider public parking garages or designated street parking areas in the city.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Villaviciosa de Córdoba — circular from Córdoba route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the spring (March to May) or autumn (September to November) when temperatures are milder. Summers in Andalusia can be extremely hot, making the demanding climbs even more challenging. Winters are usually mild but can bring rain.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route in Sierra Morena?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route, which passes through parts of the Sierra Morena region. The roads and trails are generally open for public access.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect breathtaking scenery typical of inland Andalusia. The route offers sweeping views of the valleys and passes near the Guadanuño Reservoir, providing a picturesque backdrop for your ride. Highlights include the Las Niñas Viewpoint and the Artafi Pass - Trassierra.

faqs.question: Is the Villaviciosa de Córdoba — circular from Córdoba route suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed on public roads and trails, this specific route is designed for racebikes and is very challenging. It is not typically suitable for bringing dogs, especially given the distance, elevation, and potential for unpaved sections. If you do bring a dog, ensure it can keep up and is properly secured, but it's generally not recommended for this type of strenuous cycling.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants along the route?
faqs.answer: The route traverses relatively peaceful inland roads with little traffic. While specific cafes or restaurants directly on the route are not detailed, Villaviciosa de Córdoba itself would offer options for refreshments. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer, more remote sections.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several official trails. Notable sections include parts of the Sendero de Sierra Morena, PR-A 343 Vereda de Trassierra, and SL-A 75. It also briefly touches upon [CIMA CO02] Artafi and [CIMA CO05] San Jerónimo - Las Ermitas.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a racebike trip on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring a well-maintained racebike, appropriate cycling gear, plenty of water (at least 2-3 liters), energy snacks, a repair kit (spare tubes, pump, multi-tool), a first-aid kit, sun protection, and a fully charged phone with the route downloaded. Due to potential unpaved sections, consider tires with good puncture resistance.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner cyclists or families?
faqs.answer: No, this route is explicitly classified as 'difficult' and requires 'very good fitness'. It is not suitable for beginner cyclists or families. The substantial elevation gain, long distance, and challenging terrain make it best suited for experienced road cyclists seeking a strenuous workout.

faqs.question: Are there any specific viewpoints or landmarks to look out for?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! The route features the Las Niñas Viewpoint, which offers great vistas. You'll also pass through the challenging Artafi Pass - Trassierra and cycle along the scenic Villaviciosa Road. Keep an eye out for sweeping valley views throughout the ride.
